turns.text: But how would you be any better off if you got the names of ten contributors and their addresses on the -- and I could get $252 and it just tell you would be a contribution, total receipts for the calendar year. Could you then make a better determination than that's of Justice Stewart question?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: It's entirely possible.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: What -- what about -- what is it about the names of ten people with $2 or $3 or $10 along side each that makes that easier for.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: It seems to me -- would seem a little remote with, Your Honor. I (Inaudible) a moment ago and was clear under it so that supposed the officers of the organization are in fact the only contributors to it and not, upon their coming in, reference is only newspapers, wouldn't they? And upon their coming from forces outside the organization and incidentally, most charitable organizations do lay as most of them might, sources outside membership. And then, the funds are paid out that are taken in but you don't know to whom it is paid. The organization itself, that is the (Inaudible) officers may very well be just taking that money as a commission for a solicitation which does require the payment of previous tax in Little Rock. Solicitant and for the clients.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: That -- that makes sense. Still, I don't understand how you get all of this out of a list of ten names for the $2 along side each aggregating $252?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Well, in your situation, $252 (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, this is what -- this is what they reported to you. They reported to you that the total receipts of $252 for membership of its contributors for the calendar year, 1957. They'll get it -- they spent $191.60.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The dollar (Inaudible)

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: They set the national offer to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- for the Little Rock. I don't know, in North Little Rock.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Which one is it?

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Oh, I'm sorry.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: You're reading from North Little Rock.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: I'm reading North Little Rock?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's 27.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: That is appreciable larger sum.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: $1791 here.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, that -- then you get a hundred names instead of ten.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Yes. I Suppose I can't answer the question.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Mr. Kemp.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: If Little Rock claims that any business owes but hasn't paid an occupation license. As I understood you to say earlier in answer to a question, that I asked you, they could simply assess it under the existing ordinance 7444, together with any penalties for failure to promptly apply and pay. That's been the normal remedy until the enactment of this new section in that, this new ordinance.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: And the old one still exist, isn't it?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Well, I'm sure that is (Inaudible) suggest.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Doesn't that then allow you to collect any back occupational taxes, that are -- do you? You assess them at whatever figure you think is right?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Not under our order, Your Honor. No, sir. We have that -- that privilege under this ordinance. I think that the intent of this particular ordinance under consideration here that's in the -- that or the ordinance, was to aid in assessing the tax if in fact one is due but not to assess it unless they determine that one was really due and was -- and that there was an attempt to avoid to paying that.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: May I ask you if it's your idea that the ordinance you have would cover that, if they make them. At the end of the year, they have not spend as much as they made in doing what they say they do, on page 26 on the subdivision D. Do you remember what that is? Voluntarily, promoting equality of rights and eradicate as the rates. That's interested colored citizens, scope of their impartial separates and to increase their opportunities to securing justice into court, education and so forth, accepting that as correct. Is it your idea that your ordinance covers that? I don't quite understand your idea of profit.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: I -- I did not say that.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: If they do make a profit in doing that or whatever you call the profit.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Well, I -- I think that we're referring to monetary profit, Your Honor, when we talked about the --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But I understand that.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But what I'm saying is, assume that's the hold up right there.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Assuming that is the --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And that they've collected $2400 which they paid out in salaries for their people --

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: -- the officials. Is it your idea that they would go anything under your ordinance?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: I would assume not if that picture right there were in fact true -- true picture. I would assume there would be not tax.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: They would have to be engaged in something like selling goods or some business or anything.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: They would not be indeed in a profit venture. Yes, sir.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well, and suppose they make a -- what you call a profit out of that?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Well, I don't concern that -- I would not do that to be a profit and that -- and that it's paid out in salaries.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well they show us -- they show a balance on hand of $300 --

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: -- of expenditures under receipts. So they have $300 balance on hand --

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Would that be a profit?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: It very well maybe. I -- I could not say.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, assuming that is a profit based on what they have told you on the things that they do and the kind of things their engaged in. Is there anything in your ordinance that will subject that profit, what are -- and subject the organization by reason of that profit to any of these license fees under the ordinance?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: If I accept their statement, what they do as being final then I'll agree with you that there is probably (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Nothing under the ordinance?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, then if -- and if you didn't accept their statement or if you wanted them in more particularized statement, the -- you or this -- or the appropriate officials in the municipality could ask for it, couldn't it? As to a more detailed statement of how they carry on these activities?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: I came under this ordinance otherwise not.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Under this ordinance, under D of this ordinance. And no -- no such effort was made by the city was it, to get -- to get a more particularized statement of the purpose under D of the ordinance? And as -- as the statement now appears, you're telling us that even catch-all part of the ordinance, the catch-all ordinance, wouldn't cover these activities even if conducted for a profit, is that right?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: I'm saying that that's the information they revealed here as to be their activity. It -- it's taken on its face value.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: I cannot say that there is a profit made in view of the brief.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, assuming a profit. Let's assume that -- that these activities are carried on for a profit?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Well, then if it is in fact carried on for the benefit of a profit or someone to -- to make a profit, then in fact, there is tax due, yes. But this is an organizational figure here, it doesn't show that -- that anyone in particular has received it.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: But under what ordinance would the tax be due, even assuming the profit --

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: I would assume --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- carrying on these activities.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: I would assume under the catch-all ordinance.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Could you tell us the citation of that catch-all (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: It's in the ordinance itself, 7444, the original ordinance.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, is that -- is that original ordinance in the --

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: It's in the original transcript at the clerk's office.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: You mean outside the (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: That's -- that's the basic --

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Yes, sir. That's the basic ordinance.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: In the questions you asked, did it ask anything about what they've been doing in addition to this? I'm not talking about, I know it asked for the contributors, the names of their members but did it ask in what -- try to prove the kind of activity in which they have been engaged?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: It required that that information be given, yes, sir.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Where is that?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: And that information was given.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Where is that? Well, that has been given here. After this was given, did you ask anymore?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Oh, no. It was not --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Did they give you everything in this regard as to what --

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: They -- they have given -- I'm assuming that the person or persons who made the request were satisfied with the information furnished accepting as to itself Section E.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: As in contributors?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And the number?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROBERT L. CARTER
turns.text: If the Court please.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Carter.

turns.name: ROBERT L. CARTER
turns.text: I think that Mr. Kemp has mislead the Court in -- at least in my understanding in -- in answer to his question about the public, a publication of this information. My understanding of Arkansas law is that a pubic record is -- is -- that there is an entitlement on the -- the public access to it and this was set out in a decision, Collins versus State which is at 143 Southwest Second 1, in which the Supreme Court has -- has determined what is a public record. So, that I -- I think that the interested party, information that he is dealing with concerns a definition of what is an interested party and litigation and not with the kind of problem that we have here. One other thing, I would like to bring the Court's attention to and that is, the question was asked, I mean I was trying to find it. Where did it appear in the opinion of the Court that the -- this ordinance was interpreted as applying only to members as of -- as reaching membership in contributions. I might refer the Court to the top of page 71 where the -- of the record on the case where the -- in the -- in the opinion of the Court where it says that -- about the third sentence that each in prosecuting her appealed to this Court raises this federal question. The ordinance involved insofar as it requires the names and addresses of their member of and contributors to the local branch of the N.A.A.C.P. is an invasion of rights etcetera in all questions merge into this. And I might also refer the Court to page 76 further on in the discussion of the opinion, at the top of the page about, mid the -- the paragraph when the -- there's a -- the -- the Court seeks to distinguish N.A.A.C.P. versus Alabama. That says that the ultimate aim in N.A.A.C.P. versus Alabama was to stop the activities of the N.A.A.C.P. But in the case at bar, the disclosure of N.A.A.C.P. list of members and contributors is a mere incident to see if legal taxation is being evaded. So, I think that the -- this has been the -- this -- the -- this ordinance has been interpreted to reach members and contributors. Finally, I would like to point out to the Court that this 7444 as I indicated before, list various occupation under which there is this -- this occupation tax with the listing of what the amount of the tax is and the taxes on the business on the -- on the activity is not -- is indicated on any organization itself.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: What about catch-all clause, have adverted.

turns.name: ROBERT L. CARTER
turns.text: Well, the -- I don't know of any -- I haven't seen any tax of catch-all --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROBERT L. CARTER
turns.text: Yes, sir. I have a -- Section 1 reads that the carrying on of any business, profession, occupation of whatever kind or nature within the City Little Rock, Arkansas is hereby declared to be a privilege. And from here on after the effective date of this ordinance, any person, firm or corporation engaged in any business, occupation or profession of whatever kind or nature within the City of Little Rock, shall pay various tax. But then, there is a list, there is a listing of the various occupations that are subject to the tax with the gradation of what the tax is. And my feeling is that this limits --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: What if there's a conclusory provision that this is kind of a virtue that -- that the tax at all events, any business, profession or occupation, not listed to a $25 case.

turns.name: ROBERT L. CARTER
turns.text: That I don't know.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And probably, do you know that that's used in catch-all in some dues. Do you have the ordinance before you?

turns.name: ROBERT L. CARTER
turns.text: Yes, sir. I have the copy of it of -- of--

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What's the last one part of it. That's ordinarily what a catch-all provision (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROBERT L. CARTER
turns.text: Well, the last Section 14 repeals all the other ordinances as being inequitable.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, after the list of the thing --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: The last of the list of the taxable --

turns.name: ROBERT L. CARTER
turns.text: Alright. I'll look at -- I look for that. Section 2, after listing the ordinance, says that any person from a corporation selling any in all kinds of goods, where as in merchandise unless otherwise provided in Section 1 above shall pay on the 1st of January, an annual privilege tax based upon one half of 1% of the gross value of the average stock inventory for the preceding year.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: In it? I just sort of any what --

turns.name: ROBERT L. CARTER
turns.text: Any person, firm or cooperation within the City of Little Rock engaging in the business of selling any in all kinds of goods, wares and merchandise whether raw material or finished products or both from a regularly established place of business maintained within the City Little Rock, unless otherwise provided in Section 1 above, shall pay on the 1st of January of each year, an annual privilege tax for the privilege of doing business within the City, in an amount equal to one half of 1% of the gross value of the average stock inventory for the preceding year. Now --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: This -- may I interrupt you just a moment, Mr. Carter and ask Mr. Kemp. Is that the -- is that the provision you spoke of?

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: May I -- I was going to ask if I could correct the statement on this, Your Honor but I -- I do not --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes. You -- you may correct and if you'll excuse, Mr. Carter just a moment, to please --

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: In reference to that catch-all clause it is specific with -- in reference to organizations engaging in the business of selling of goods, wares and merchandise, whether raw materials or finished product or both, says that in no instance shall any such activity being privileged to do business more or less than $25 of the tax.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, that's --

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: That's (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: As merchandise.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: That's -- that was affected to a merchandise, yes. It would not reach this ordinance.

turns.name: JOSEPH C. KEMP
turns.text: That -- that catch-all and I would -- I just -- just doesn't -- I wanted to --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Now, if you may, Mr. Carter you may --

turns.name: ROBERT L. CARTER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- you may proceed again.

turns.name: ROBERT L. CARTER
turns.text: Well, I think I'm -- well, if the Court please, we -- we contend that this -- that -- that the ordinance insofar as it has been applied to us as unconstitutional and that insofar as it seeks to get out membership list for some constitutionality of the convictions hereunder cannot stand.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: We'll recess now.
